reflection.quote: The most regretful people on earth are those who felt the call to creative work, who felt their own creative power restive and uprising, and gave to it neither power nor time.
reflection.reading:
Sunday in late May. The kind of day that used to disappear under a hangover — curtains drawn, phone face-down, the whole afternoon spent negotiating with your own body. You'd surface around four, eat something beige, promise yourself Monday would be different. It rarely was.

What nobody tells you about getting sober is how much Sunday you get back. Not the Instagram version — brunch and farmers markets and curated calm. The real version. A slow coffee that you actually taste. A walk where your legs work and your head is clear enough to notice the light coming through the trees. A phone call to someone you love that you don't have to perform through. The afternoon stretches out in front of you instead of closing in.

Mary Oliver was talking about creative work, but I think the same thing applies to a life. You can feel the call of it — the version of you that wants to build something, show up for people, be present for your own existence — and you can give it nothing. Or you can give it this Sunday. Not a grand gesture. Just the ordinary hours, used. The walk taken. The book opened. The text sent. This is what sobriety actually buys you: not a better life in some abstract future, but a real one, today, in the hours you used to throw away.
reflection.meditation: What would I do with this Sunday if I trusted it was mine to spend?

affirmations: I am awake for my own life today, and that is not a small thing.
Today I trade the wasted afternoon for the lived one.
I have the clarity, the time, and the agency to use this day well.

gratitude.prayer: Grateful for a Sunday I can remember. Grateful for the quiet that used to terrify me and now feels like a gift. Grateful for the slow morning, the clear head, the people in my life who get the version of me that shows up sober. Grateful for the years that taught me what this day is worth.
gratitude.closing: One more day, used well. That's the whole thing.
